# file to edit: _notebooks/2021-01-18-Five-Levels-of-Search.ipynb
-->

<div class="container" id="notebook-container">
        
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Unstructured data contains much valuable knowledge. However, the problem with it is that it is hard to access. Since there's no structure, you can't just grab what you want and move on.</p>
<p>One of the most important forms of unstructured data is text. To find useful information from text we need a search engine.</p>
<p>A search engine that anyone is familiar with is a web search engine such as Google. If you think about it, the fact that you can search the entire web for information is terrific.</p>
<p>There are hundreds of terabytes of raw text on the internet, yet somehow, you can find exactly what you are looking for by picking the right combination of keywords. That is if you are able to choose them.</p>
<p>Web search engines have a significant advantage in relevancy. Namely, it can take advantage of user and website data.</p>
<p>For the former, it has information on what people search, what they click on, how long until they search again, where they are from, what their interests are.</p>
<p>It is possible for the latter to rank websites according to their linking structure, user engagement, loading speed, and more.</p>
<p>All of this information is cleverly used, to produce highly relevant search results, even while having a superficial understanding of the knowledge that's returned.</p>
<p>As an example, Google knows that I often use programming related queries.</p>
<p>So do many other people that also like to click on <a href="https://stackoverflow.com/">Stackoverflow</a> links. Say there is evidence that 90% of people who are similar to me, click on a particular Stackoverflow link for a specific search term.</p>
<p>It makes sense to rank this result high, even if there is no <em>understanding</em> of relevancy on a content level.</p>
<p>Some keywords will match, and that's great, but that is far from why the result was given a high ranking.</p>
<p>Using user data is just one of the many ways a search engine can provide relevant results. Most companies and people don't have such luxuries.</p>
<p>There is no silver bullet for the problem. The only solution is building an exceptional search engine that can understand what we want to find.</p>
<p>In the following five levels of search, we will build an intelligent search engine from scratch to gain a deeper understanding of the problem.</p>
<p>Ultimately, we will see how the problem can be quickly solved using open-source libraries and what marvels await us in the future of search.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h2 id="Levels-of-search">Levels of search<a class="anchor-link" href="#Levels-of-search"> </a></h2><p>Search is an significant enough field to have various solutions and software offerings for many different domains. To cut through the noise, I will be focusing on the fundamentals of text-based search. Each level will show the code behind the implementation. You may comfortably skip over the code and still build an intuitive understanding of the topic.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h3 id="Level-1---Keyword-matching">Level 1 - Keyword matching<a class="anchor-link" href="#Level-1---Keyword-matching"> </a></h3><p>Starting from the very basics, we can search using keywords. It is straightforward to come up with an algorithm that does just that.</p>
<p>The idea is to split every document into words, count occurrences and save the processed version, thus "indexing" it.</p>
<p>Once we query for a document, we will do the same processing on the query and count exact keyword matches, thus "scoring" documents.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">doc_1</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">&quot;Funny videos about cats&quot;</span>
<span class="n">doc_2</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">&quot;Cats, cats, cats!&quot;</span>
<span class="n">doc_3</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">&quot;silly dog videos&quot;</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Natural language is messy, so even at level 1, we should do some cleaning. Let's write a simple function to get keywords from text.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">text_to_keywords</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">document</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="c1"># Turn to lowercase</span>
    <span class="n">text</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">lower</span><span class="p">()</span>
    <span class="c1"># Remove special characters</span>
    <span class="n">text</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">re</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">sub</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">r</span><span class="s2">&quot;[^a-z0-9 ]&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">text</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="c1"># Split to keywords on space</span>
    <span class="n">kws</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">text</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">split</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot; &quot;</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">kws</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">level1_index_documents</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">document</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="c1"># Get list of keywords</span>
        <span class="n">kws</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">text_to_keywords</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">document</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="c1"># Count keywords</span>
        <span class="n">counts</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">collections</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">Counter</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="c1"># Nice index representation</span>
        <span class="n">indexed_document</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;doc&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;counts&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="nb">dict</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">counts</span><span class="p">)}</span>
        <span class="c1"># Add to our index</span>
        <span class="n">index</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">append</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">indexed_document</span><span class="p">)</span>
    
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">index</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">level1_index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">level1_index_documents</span><span class="p">([</span><span class="n">doc_1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_2</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_3</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>[{&#39;doc&#39;: &#39;Funny videos about cats&#39;,
  &#39;counts&#39;: {&#39;funny&#39;: 1, &#39;videos&#39;: 1, &#39;about&#39;: 1, &#39;cats&#39;: 1}},
 {&#39;doc&#39;: &#39;Cats, cats, cats!&#39;, &#39;counts&#39;: {&#39;cats&#39;: 3}},
 {&#39;doc&#39;: &#39;silly dog videos&#39;, &#39;counts&#39;: {&#39;silly&#39;: 1, &#39;dog&#39;: 1, &#39;videos&#39;: 1}}]</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>What we accomplished is counting how many times a keyword occurs in a document. That's about all we need to start searching for our documents.</p>
<p>The final piece is a ranking function.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">level1_rank</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="c1"># Split query to keywords</span>
    <span class="n">kws</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">text_to_keywords</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">document</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="mf">0.0</span>
        <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">kw</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">:</span>
            <span class="c1"># Add the count of keywords as the score</span>
            <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">+=</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;counts&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kw</span><span class="p">)</span> <span class="ow">or</span> <span class="mf">0.0</span>
        
        <span class="n">result</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;doc&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;score&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">score</span><span class="p">}</span>
        <span class="n">results</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">append</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">result</span><span class="p">)</span>
    
    <span class="c1"># Sort results</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="nb">sorted</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">results</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">key</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="k">lambda</span> <span class="n">x</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">x</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;score&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">reverse</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="kc">True</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">results</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Ranking simply adds a score of <code>1</code> every time a query keyword matches with a document keyword.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">rank_func</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">rank_func</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">result</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">results</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">f</span><span class="s2">&quot;</span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">result</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s1">&#39;doc&#39;</span><span class="p">][</span><span class="s1">&#39;doc&#39;</span><span class="p">]</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s2"> </span><span class="se">\t</span><span class="s2"> </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">result</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s1">&#39;score&#39;</span><span class="p">]</span><span class="si">:</span><span class="s2">.4f</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s2">&quot;</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">expandtabs</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="mi">30</span><span class="p">))</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>After implementing a nice wrapper, we can start searching. Let us try to search for <code>cats</code>.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cats&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level1_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level1_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Cats, cats, cats!              3.0000
Funny videos about cats        1.0000
silly dog videos               0.0000
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Great! The two documents that mention cats are ranked at the top, while <code>dog videos</code> gets a score of <code>0.0</code>.</p>
<p>Search solved?</p>
<p>Unfortunately not, far from it.</p>
<p>If you are paying attention, I am sure you have noticed a big red flag.</p>
<p>The first document has a score that's <code>3x</code> higher than the second result. All because it has "stuffed" the keyword <code>cats</code>.</p>
<p>This brings us back to the early days of search engines, where people could rank pages high just by "stuffing" their content with repeated keywords.</p>
<p>One such strategy involved writing the excess keywords in white font on a white background so only the indexing robots could read it.</p>
<p>Clearly, this is not a great solution.</p>
<p>Let's look at another example to see what else is wrong with this approach.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat videos&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level1_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level1_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Funny videos about cats        1.0000
silly dog videos               1.0000
Cats, cats, cats!              0.0000
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>As you may have expected, <code>silly dog videos</code> and <code>cat videos</code> get precisely the same score.</p>
<p>This is because our simple indexing system cannot understand that singular and plural forms of words are the same thing. The only keyword that matches is <code>videos</code>.</p>
<p>All in all, these two red flags make this approach uninteresting. However, we did learn about two essential search concepts: indexing and scoring.</p>
<p><strong>Indexing</strong> is processing and saving documents to make them easily scorable/retrievable for search queries.</p>
<p><strong>Scoring</strong> is the process of giving documents a score based on the query and information in the index.</p>
<p>These are the two crucial pieces that make every search engine work.</p>
<p>While it is possible to score documents without indexing, it makes searching too slow for large amounts of documents and does not support some of the advanced scoring algorithms.</p>
<p>As we climb towards higher levels, it is essential to remember that indexing and scoring is all a search engine is doing on a fundamental level.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h3 id="Level-2---Token-matching-algorithms">Level 2 - Token matching algorithms<a class="anchor-link" href="#Level-2---Token-matching-algorithms"> </a></h3>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Level 1 is a dumb search engine with two big problems:</p>
<ol>
<li>ranking by keyword count, thus enabling keyword stuffing</li>
<li>scoring documents only by exact keywords, therefore disregarding plural forms</li>
</ol>
<p>To remedy problem 1, we will turn the words to tokens in a way that captures singular, plural forms and more. Allowing for usage on languages that do not contain any spaces, such as Chinese.</p>
<p>Problem 2 can be fixed using the <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Okapi_BM25">BM25 scoring algorithm</a>, one of the most popular algorithms in modern open-source search engines such as <a href="https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch">Elasticsearch</a> and <a href="https://lucene.apache.org/solr/">Solr</a>.</p>
<p>In Level 1 of search, those tokens were words, and the scoring counted the number of words. In Level 2 of search, the tokens will be parts of words, and our scoring function will severely penalise keyword stuffing.</p>
<p>Our resulting approach will be comparable to most search engines widely used today (with a few tweaks here and there).</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h4 id="Token-indexing">Token indexing<a class="anchor-link" href="#Token-indexing"> </a></h4><p>We will clean text and split it to words, same as before. As an additional step, we'll make <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/N-gram">n-grams</a> from words. As an example, an n-gram of size 3 (trigram) will split a word to sequences of 3 characters.</p>
<p>This idea is best conveyed through an example. The word <code>machine</code> would result in 5 different tokens: <code>mac</code>, <code>ach</code>, <code>chi</code>, <code>hin</code>, <code>ine</code>. Using this approach, <code>cats</code> would become <code>cat</code> and <code>ats</code>.</p>
<p>The trick is to apply the same tokenisation to queries, meaning that similar-looking words will be scored higher and exact matches will still be ranked highest.</p>
<p>Not only will this help with singular and plural forms, but also with compound words that are very common in some languages like German and Estonian.
As an example, <code>face mask</code> translated to Estonian is <code>näomask</code>, where <code>näo</code> is a grammar case of face and <code>mask</code> is mask.</p>
<p>Hopefully, you see that if I were to search <code>mask</code> in Estonian using our Level 1 approach, I would not find any results about face masks. However, I would get a match if I transformed both the document <code>näomask</code> and the query <code>mask</code> into trigrams.</p>
<p><code>näomask =&gt; näo, äom, oma, mas, ask</code></p>
<p><code>mask =&gt; mas, ask</code></p>
<p>This approach would give two token matches to my query.</p>
<p>Let's write quick n-gram tokenisation and indexing functions that achieve just that.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">kws_to_ngram_tokens</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">ngram</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">ngram_tokens</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">kw</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="n">tokens</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[</span><span class="n">kw</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="n">i</span><span class="p">:</span><span class="n">i</span><span class="o">+</span><span class="n">ngram</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">i</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="nb">range</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="nb">len</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kw</span><span class="p">)</span> <span class="o">-</span> <span class="n">ngram</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="mi">1</span><span class="p">)]</span>
        <span class="n">ngram_tokens</span> <span class="o">+=</span> <span class="n">tokens</span>
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">ngram_tokens</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">kws_to_ngram_tokens</span><span class="p">([</span><span class="s2">&quot;näomask&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="mi">3</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>[&#39;näo&#39;, &#39;äom&#39;, &#39;oma&#39;, &#39;mas&#39;, &#39;ask&#39;]</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">level2_index_documents</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">ngram</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mi">3</span><span class="p">):</span> 
    <span class="n">index_docs</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="n">total_len</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="mi">0</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">document</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="c1"># Get list of keywords</span>
        <span class="n">kws</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">text_to_keywords</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">document</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="c1"># Tokenize keywords</span>
        <span class="n">toks</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">kws_to_ngram_tokens</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">ngram</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="c1"># Get length of all tokens</span>
        <span class="n">length</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="nb">len</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">toks</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="n">total_len</span> <span class="o">+=</span> <span class="n">length</span>
        <span class="c1"># Count tokens</span>
        <span class="n">counts</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">collections</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">Counter</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">toks</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="c1"># Nice index representation</span>
        <span class="n">indexed_document</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;doc&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;counts&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="nb">dict</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">counts</span><span class="p">),</span> <span class="s2">&quot;length&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">length</span><span class="p">}</span>
        <span class="c1"># Add to our index</span>
        <span class="n">index_docs</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">append</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">indexed_document</span><span class="p">)</span>
    
    <span class="c1"># Average length of documents</span>
    <span class="n">avg_len</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">total_len</span> <span class="o">/</span> <span class="nb">len</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="n">index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span>
        <span class="s2">&quot;avg_length&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">avg_len</span><span class="p">,</span>
        <span class="s2">&quot;num_docs&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="nb">len</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">),</span>
        <span class="s2">&quot;docs&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">index_docs</span>
    <span class="p">}</span>
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">index</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Almost there! We've solved one problem. Besides tokenising keywords, we added some extra information to the index. It will make sense why once we get an overview of the ranking algorithm.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">doc_4</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">&quot;viral triple rainbow clip&quot;</span>
<span class="n">level2_index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">level2_index_documents</span><span class="p">([</span><span class="n">doc_1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_2</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_3</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_4</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h4 id="BM25-ranking">BM25 ranking<a class="anchor-link" href="#BM25-ranking"> </a></h4><p>Understanding every small detail of the ranking algorithm is not very important.</p>
<p>It is merely necessary to understand the intuition behind the algorithm. There are methods out there that do an excellent job at finding relevant information according to our query terms.</p>
<p>If you don't want to focus on the math (which I completely understand), here's what you need to know.</p>
<p>Our algorithm will value each token differently, based on how common it is. Additionally, repeated tokens become less important, the more of them there are.</p>
<p>If you are interested in the specifics, here is an adapted summary from <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Okapi_BM25">wikipedia</a>:</p>
$$
\text{score}(D,Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IDF}(q_i) \cdot \frac{f(q_i, D) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(q_i, D) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{|D|}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}
$$<p>In the BM25 algorithm, $f(q_i, D)$ is the frequency of tokens in a document, which rewards token repetitions. $|D|$ is the number of tokens in a document. $avgdl$ is the average length of all documents. $k_1$ and $b$ are tweakable parameters which penalise or reward token frequencies.</p>
<p>Thus, the numerator in the fraction boosts the document score for repeated tokens. And the denominator penalises the document score for repeated tokens, scaling by document length.</p>
<p>If the document is particularly long, it is penalised more as it has more "opportunities" for matching tokens.</p>
<p>$IDF(q_i)$ calculates inverse document frequency of the token and is computed as:</p>
$$
\text{IDF}(q_i) = \ln (\frac{N - n(q_i) + 0.5}{n(q_i) + 0.5}+1)
$$<p>Here, $N$ is the total number of documents and $n(q_i)$ is the number of document containing the token $(q_i)$.</p>
<p>Inverse document frequency allows us to treat common tokens as less important. English documents contain a lot of "the" words in documents.</p>
<p>The word itself has very little meaning and thus should not contribute a lot to the final score.</p>
<p>The final score is summed over all query tokens. Let's implement it.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">level2_rank</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">ngram</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mi">3</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">k1</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mf">1.2</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">b</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mf">0.75</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="c1"># Split query to keywords</span>
    <span class="n">kws</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">text_to_keywords</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="c1"># Get tokens from keywords</span>
    <span class="n">toks</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">kws_to_ngram_tokens</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">ngram</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">document</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;docs&quot;</span><span class="p">]:</span>
        <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="mf">0.0</span>
        <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">tok</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">toks</span><span class="p">:</span>
            <span class="c1"># Token frequency in document</span>
            <span class="n">freq</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;counts&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">tok</span><span class="p">)</span> <span class="ow">or</span> <span class="mi">0</span>
            <span class="c1"># Number of documents with token</span>
            <span class="n">num_docs_with_tok</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="nb">len</span><span class="p">([</span><span class="mi">1</span> <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">doc</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;docs&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="k">if</span> <span class="n">doc</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;counts&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">tok</span><span class="p">)])</span>
            <span class="n">idf</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">math</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">log</span><span class="p">((</span><span class="n">index</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;num_docs&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">-</span> <span class="n">num_docs_with_tok</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="mf">0.5</span><span class="p">)</span>
                           <span class="o">/</span> <span class="p">(</span><span class="n">num_docs_with_tok</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="mf">0.5</span><span class="p">)</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="mi">1</span><span class="p">)</span>
        
            <span class="n">numerator</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">freq</span> <span class="o">*</span> <span class="p">(</span><span class="n">k1</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="mi">1</span><span class="p">)</span>
            <span class="n">denominator</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">freq</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="n">k1</span> <span class="o">*</span> <span class="p">(</span><span class="mi">1</span> <span class="o">-</span> <span class="n">b</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="n">b</span> <span class="o">*</span> <span class="p">(</span><span class="n">document</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;length&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">/</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;avg_length&quot;</span><span class="p">]))</span>
            
            <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">+=</span> <span class="n">idf</span> <span class="o">*</span> <span class="p">(</span><span class="n">numerator</span> <span class="o">/</span> <span class="n">denominator</span><span class="p">)</span>
        
        <span class="n">result</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;doc&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;score&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">score</span><span class="p">}</span>
        <span class="n">results</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">append</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">result</span><span class="p">)</span>
    
    <span class="c1"># Sort results</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="nb">sorted</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">results</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">key</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="k">lambda</span> <span class="n">x</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">x</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;score&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">reverse</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="kc">True</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">results</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>The ranking of Level 2 is not very different from Level 1. Instead of keywords, we use tokens. We no longer count occurences but calculate the score with BM25.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Cats, cats, cats!              1.1913
Funny videos about cats        0.6407
silly dog videos               0.0000
viral triple rainbow clip      0.0000
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>We've managed to match plural and singular forms! However, we can see that the stuffed document still ranks higher, but we're already doing better!</p>
<p>The difference is less than <code>2x</code> compared to <code>3x</code> with the level 1 approach.</p>
<p>Admittedly, the query is a bit ambiguous. It isn't clear that we are trying to find videos.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat vid&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Funny videos about cats        1.2814
Cats, cats, cats!              1.1913
silly dog videos               0.7549
viral triple rainbow clip      0.0000
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>By being more specific, we can see that we finally beat keyword stuffing.</p>
<p>Using the level 1 ranking with our new tokenisation would still rank the stuffed document higher.</p>
<p>This is because <code>cat vid</code> only matches two tokens from <code>funny videos about cats</code>, but would match three from <code>cats cats cats</code> (remember the trigrams).</p>
<p>BM25 is "smart" enough to weigh the <code>cat</code> token as lower value, since it is more common.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat videos&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Funny videos about cats        3.2036
silly dog videos               3.0197
Cats, cats, cats!              1.1913
viral triple rainbow clip      0.0000
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>By doing a less slangy search term, we can see that it is now clearly ahead.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;hats hats hats&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Cats, cats, cats!              3.5740
Funny videos about cats        1.9222
silly dog videos               0.0000
viral triple rainbow clip      0.0000
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Uh-oh... What happened here?</p>
<p>Well, since we use trigrams, cats and hats are equivalent when searching <code>cat</code> vs <code>hats</code>. The former matches the <code>cat</code> trigram, while the latter matches the <code>ats</code> trigram.</p>
<p>This can be fixed by playing around with n-gram lengths or using different tokenisation altogether. Additionally, since our index is tiny, we don't have anything about <code>hats</code>. We got a false positive, and it's not the end of the world.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;animal clips&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>viral triple rainbow clip      2.0693
Funny videos about cats        0.0000
Cats, cats, cats!              0.0000
silly dog videos               0.0000
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>This case is much worse as we have both a false positive and two false negatives. Our level 2 search engine is not "smart" enough to understand that <code>clip</code> is synonymous to <code>video</code> and both <code>dogs</code> and <code>cats</code> are <code>animals</code>.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;bow hunting&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level2_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>viral triple rainbow clip      1.0347
Funny videos about cats        0.0000
Cats, cats, cats!              0.0000
silly dog videos               0.0000
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Remember the example with "näomask" (face mask)? Well, it turns out token matching for compound words is a double-edged sword. We are matching the token <code>bow</code> in <code>rainbow</code>, and this is giving us another false positive.</p>
<p>In fact, scoring is a huge issue in general.</p>
<p>It is not too hard to have a reasonably good ranking between results.</p>
<p>What's much harder is figuring out whether there are any relevant results at all. You can't set a hard limit that says "anything below a score of 1 or 2 is not relevant". In practice, the scores can vary a lot from query to query, and from my own research and testing, there is no right way to tell.</p>
<p>If you try to think like an engineer, I'm sure you can figure out plenty of fixes to the problems.</p>
<p>For example, to get around the <code>clip</code> vs <code>video</code> problem, you could index them as synonyms. Most modern search engines support this feature, and it is not too hard to implement in our small example either.</p>
<p>And truth be told, I could play around with some parameters and index more documents to better represent a real-world scenario.</p>
<p>Here you can see that achieving good search performance in a particular domain can become a significant tweaking/engineering task - and there is incredible work being done in this area.</p>
<p>Even if we manage to hack our solution to be "perfect", there are no guarantees that it will be perfect tomorrow when the index is updated. And indeed, there is no guarantee of perfection at all since you can't possibly cover every single query.</p>
<p>The whole point of a search engine is to search for whatever you want to find.</p>
<p>All of the issues arise from a fundamental problem: there is no <em>understanding</em> in our search engine.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h3 id="Level-3---Word-level-semantics">Level 3 - Word level semantics<a class="anchor-link" href="#Level-3---Word-level-semantics"> </a></h3>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>How do we even begin introducing understanding to our search engine?</p>
<p>If you've been listening to the buzz, you may think of AI.</p>
<p>And that is indeed the correct answer.</p>
<p>However, it is misleading and not helpful to label everything remotely intelligent as AI. There are many different approaches in AI that can be applied to various problems.</p>
<p>It is useful to build an intuition about them so that, so you could understand their capabilities and limitations.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h4 id="Understanding-and-indexing-keywords">Understanding and indexing keywords<a class="anchor-link" href="#Understanding-and-indexing-keywords"> </a></h4><p>The key to making a computer understand words is to turn the words into an appropriate form that makes them easy to manipulate through calculations.</p>
<p>You may have guessed that this is numeric form.</p>
<p>The basic idea behind achieving this is to let the computer learn the "numbers" for each word. The way this can be done is quite clever.</p>
<p>One approach is to feed it a bunch of text, say the entirety of Wikipedia. You can instruct the algorithm to predict the next word, given the previous words it has seen.</p>
<p>To do this task well, it must learn about relationships between words.</p>
<p>For example, if I asked you to complete the sentence "He was walking his ___", I'm sure you could come up with a reasonable answer.</p>
<p>The only way the computer can do it well, is by learning to represent words coherently.</p>
<p>These numeric word representations are called word vectors or embeddings.</p>
<p>At this time, I will not show how to train these vectors from scratch, but I may write another post about it in the future. If you're interested in knowing more now, search for "language modelling" and "learning word embeddings".</p>
<p>For now, you can take my word that it is possible. Let's take some pre-trained vectors from <a href="https://fasttext.cc">fasttext</a>. Visualising them shows some fantastic results.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_png output_subarea ">
<img src="data:image/png;base64,
"
>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Here, we've done principal component analysis (PCA) to turn our word vectors from 300 dimensions to 2. This means that if our previous vectors consisted of 300 numbers each, now they consist of just 2.</p>
<p>We obviously lose a lot of information in the process, but we can at least visualise them in a 2D plot.</p>
<p>You can see that related words are close to each other, while unrelated words are farther away. Due to PCA, the distances aren't very accurately represented, but hopefully, you can see that there is a level of understanding behind the vectors.</p>
<p>To further demonstrate this, I'll show a popular example of doing arithmetic with words.</p>
<p>What is <code>king - man + woman</code>?</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">vec1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">vec2</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="nb">round</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="mi">1</span> <span class="o">-</span> <span class="n">spatial</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">distance</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">cosine</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">vec1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">vec2</span><span class="p">),</span> <span class="mi">4</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">calc_queen</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;king&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">-</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;man&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;woman&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span>
<span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">calc_queen</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;queen&quot;</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>0.6543</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Using <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosine_similarity">cosine similarity</a> to compare vectors, we get a result of roughly <code>0.65</code>. As cosine similarity ranges from -1 to 1, this means the vectors are very similar.</p>
<p>It turns out that <code>king - man + woman</code> is roughly equal to queen. Very cool!</p>
<p>To verify this and see if this approach could help us with cats, dogs, animals and everything else we've been trying we can make more comparisons.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;cats&quot;</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>0.835</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;cats&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;dogs&quot;</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>0.7548</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>As we hoped, <code>cat</code> is similar to <code>cats</code>, which is similar to <code>dogs</code>, but less so. Looking good!</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;animal&quot;</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>0.5026</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;skyscraper&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;animal&quot;</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>0.0665</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>And as expected, <code>cat</code> is similar to <code>animal</code>, while <code>skyscraper</code> isn't.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;vids&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">word_vecs</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;videos&quot;</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>0.7223</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>As a final test, we can see that it also handles slang. Looks promising. Let's write an indexing function for keyword vectors.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">level3_index_documents</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">document</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="c1"># Get list of keywords</span>
        <span class="n">kws</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">text_to_keywords</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">document</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="c1"># Count number of keywords</span>
        <span class="n">length</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="nb">len</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="c1"># Count all keywords</span>
        <span class="n">counts</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="nb">dict</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">collections</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">Counter</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">))</span>
        <span class="n">kw_vector_counts</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="n">kw</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;count&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">count</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;vector&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">ft</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get_word_vector</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kw</span><span class="p">)}</span>
                            <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">kw</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">count</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">counts</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">items</span><span class="p">()}</span>
        
        <span class="c1"># Nice index representation</span>
        <span class="n">indexed_document</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;doc&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;vector_counts&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">kw_vector_counts</span><span class="p">,</span>
                           <span class="s2">&quot;length&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">length</span><span class="p">}</span>
        <span class="c1"># Add to our index</span>
        <span class="n">index</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">append</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">indexed_document</span><span class="p">)</span>
    
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">index</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>All that we have done for indexing is saving the keyword vectors alongside their counts.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">level3_index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">level3_index_documents</span><span class="p">([</span><span class="n">doc_1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_2</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_3</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_4</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">level3_rank</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="c1"># Split query to keywords</span>
    <span class="n">kws</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">text_to_keywords</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="c1"># Get vectors for each keyword in query</span>
    <span class="n">query_kw_vectors</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[</span><span class="n">ft</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get_word_vector</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">kw</span><span class="p">)</span> <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">kw</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">kws</span><span class="p">]</span>
    <span class="c1"># Iterate over all documents</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">document</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="mf">0.0</span>
        <span class="c1"># Iterate over all query keyword vectors</span>
        <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">query_kw_vector</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">query_kw_vectors</span><span class="p">:</span>
            <span class="c1"># Iterate over all document keyword vectors</span>
            <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">_</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">vector_counts</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;vector_counts&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">items</span><span class="p">():</span>
                <span class="n">doc_kw_vector</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">vector_counts</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;vector&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span>
                <span class="n">doc_kw_count</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">vector_counts</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;count&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span>
                
                <span class="c1"># Weigh repeated document keywords</span>
                <span class="c1"># as exponentially less important</span>
                <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">+=</span> <span class="n">math</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">log</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">doc_kw_count</span> <span class="o">+</span> <span class="mi">2</span><span class="p">)</span> \
                       <span class="o">*</span> <span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query_kw_vector</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_kw_vector</span><span class="p">)</span>
        
        <span class="c1"># Normalize score over keyword * document lenghts</span>
        <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">/</span> <span class="nb">len</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query_kw_vectors</span><span class="p">)</span> <span class="o">/</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;length&quot;</span><span class="p">]</span>
        
        <span class="n">result</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;doc&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;score&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">score</span><span class="p">}</span>
        <span class="n">results</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">append</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">result</span><span class="p">)</span>
    
    <span class="c1"># Sort results</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="nb">sorted</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">results</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">key</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="k">lambda</span> <span class="n">x</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">x</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;score&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">reverse</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="kc">True</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">results</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>For the ranking, we weigh repeated keywords as exponentially less important.</p>
<p>We use cosine similarity between each of the query and document keywords and sum up the score that's normalised over query and document length.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;animal clips&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>silly dog videos               0.3430
Funny videos about cats        0.2856
viral triple rainbow clip      0.2360
Cats, cats, cats!              0.1553
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Perfect, searching for "animal clips" gives us cat and dog videos as the top result, even though they have no words or tokens in common.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;hats hats hats&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>silly dog videos               0.1921
Funny videos about cats        0.1849
Cats, cats, cats!              0.1387
viral triple rainbow clip      0.1334
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Since we are not relying on n-grams anymore, we don't make a blunder here. Notice that the top scores are considerably lower than for the last search.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;how to build a skyscraper&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Funny videos about cats        0.1323
viral triple rainbow clip      0.1170
silly dog videos               0.1015
Cats, cats, cats!              0.0300
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>A completely unrelated query gives us low scores. Let's index another document to try something a bit more challenging.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">doc_5</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">&quot;footage about toy animals&quot;</span>
<span class="n">level3_index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">level3_index_documents</span><span class="p">([</span><span class="n">doc_1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_2</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_3</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_4</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_5</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;videos of animals&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>footage about toy animals      0.2993
Funny videos about cats        0.2956
silly dog videos               0.2845
Cats, cats, cats!              0.1547
viral triple rainbow clip      0.1464
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Here's our first problem. As our similarity matching is purely on a word-level, we get an irrelevant result as the top result. <code>videos</code> matches well with <code>footage</code> and <code>animals</code> matches perfectly with <code>animals</code>.</p>
<p>Due to word-level limitations, we can't tell that <code>toy animals</code> and <code>animals</code> are very different.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat videos&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level3_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>silly dog videos               0.4435
Funny videos about cats        0.4062
footage about toy animals      0.2861
Cats, cats, cats!              0.2778
viral triple rainbow clip      0.2238
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Can you figure out what's happening here?</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">ft</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get_word_vector</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat&quot;</span><span class="p">),</span> <span class="n">ft</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get_word_vector</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;dog&quot;</span><span class="p">))</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>0.7079</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">ft</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get_word_vector</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat&quot;</span><span class="p">),</span> <span class="n">ft</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">get_word_vector</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cats&quot;</span><span class="p">))</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">



<div class="output_text output_subarea output_execute_result">
<pre>0.835</pre>
</div>

</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>It seems that <code>cat</code> is more similar to <code>cats</code> than to <code>dog</code>. That's good. The only reason for the worse score can be due to the other words in the documents.</p>
<p>In specific, <code>funny</code> and <code>about</code> are dragging the average score down more than <code>silly</code>.</p>
<p>What now?</p>
<p>We've managed to introduce understanding into our search engine.</p>
<p>We can now get away without any n-gram tokenisation and get synonym support out of the box. Additionally, we have a better way of detecting whether there are any relevant results.</p>
<p>We could say that anything below the score of <code>0.15-0.20</code> is not relevant. Whereas with the previous level, the scoring was much more arbitrary.</p>
<p>There's another problem which hasn't been mentioned, and that's scale. The current implementation needs to save word vectors for every keyword. For ranking, we need to compare every single query keyword with each document keyword for every available document.</p>
<p>Sure, we can do efficiency tricks here and there, but our engine still relies entirely on vector comparisons. That's bad for scale, both in terms of space and time.</p>
<p>Our level 2 token matching engine was much more efficient (and it can still be improved).</p>
<p>We could quickly access the relevant tokens and have a lot of pre-processed information about every document.</p>
<p>Here, for level 3, we depend on comparing a lot of different vectors, since we can't rely on exact matches.</p>
<p>While we improved on relevancy through understanding, we took a big hit on scalability.</p>
<p>This is a no go.</p>
<p>Search becomes more critical the more documents you have. Speaking of more documents, I'm reasonably confident that the BM25 ranking algorithm would perform better in most real-world scenarios.</p>
<p>Large amounts of text introduce a lot of "noise", which BM25 is built to handle.</p>
<p>It's possible to merge the two approaches, but it's time to climb a level higher.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h3 id="Level-4---Sentence-to-document-level-semantics">Level 4 - Sentence to document level semantics<a class="anchor-link" href="#Level-4---Sentence-to-document-level-semantics"> </a></h3>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>With Level 4, we are trying to solve the limitations of word only semantics and build a more scalable system.</p>
<p>It's time to ditch word vectors and use techniques to capture accurate vectors about sentences, paragraphs and entire documents. Not all AI is created equal.</p>
<p>Say we could produce a single vector for our query and every document. We could then compare our query with every available document just once, giving us linear time and space complexities.</p>
<p>Great!</p>
