<p>And if that's not good enough, there are appropriate vector search libraries out there, such as <a href="https://github.com/spotify/annoy">ANNOY</a>. If a single vector is enough to capture the meaning/information of queries and documents, then we're well off!</p>
<p>Perhaps surprisingly, the implementation for Level 4 is as easy as Level 1, that is, if you don't need to implement the language model from scratch.</p>
<p>Luckily, for the past few years, there has been a considerable amount of research in this area (and it is still ongoing).</p>
<p>One of the most important models in this area is <a href="https://github.com/google-research/bert">BERT</a>, created and released in 2018 by Google.</p>
<p>And since October 2020, it is used for all English queries in Google search. What makes it different from our word vector approach is that it takes context into account.</p>
<p>Consider two sentences:</p>
<ol>
<li>She is standing on the river bank.</li>
<li>She is opening a bank account.</li>
</ol>
<p>Clearly, the word <code>bank</code> has two different meanings in the sentences.</p>
<p>If you only have a single vector per word, you can't possibly represent two different meanings accurately.</p>
<p>Additionally, BERT can consider meaning in both directions, so it is called a bidirectional model.</p>
<p>In plain English, this means that BERT looks at the entire sentence before making the final prediction.</p>
<p>Remember my proposed approach for learning word vectors?</p>
<p>It involved trying to predict the next word given a sequence of words.</p>
<p>BERT is trained differently; it is asked to predict words within sentences, such as:</p>
<p>"The man went to the ___. He bought a ___ of milk."</p>
<p>It does some other tricks, but that's basically it. Thanks to the training and its architecture, it can represent entire sentences and documents as vectors.</p>
<p>Additionally, it is relatively quick and inexpensive to finetune, which means adapting it to your specific domain.</p>
<p>This has created an explosion of models and libraries built around BERT. While I will not show how to train a similar model from scratch, nor how to finetune BERT (though both would be fun and blog-worthy), we will cover how easy existing models are to use.</p>
<p>It's time to build our Level 4 search engine.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="kn">from</span> <span class="nn">sentence_transformers</span> <span class="kn">import</span> <span class="n">SentenceTransformer</span>
<span class="n">model</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">SentenceTransformer</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;distilroberta-base-paraphrase-v1&quot;</span><span class="p">)</span>

<span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">level4_index_documents</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">document</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="c1"># Calculate vector for document</span>
        <span class="n">vector</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">model</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">encode</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">document</span><span class="p">)</span>
        <span class="c1"># Nice index representation</span>
        <span class="n">indexed_document</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;doc&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;vector&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">vector</span><span class="p">}</span>
        <span class="c1"># Add to our index</span>
        <span class="n">index</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">append</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">indexed_document</span><span class="p">)</span>
    
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">index</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">level4_index</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">level4_index_documents</span><span class="p">([</span><span class="n">doc_1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_2</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_3</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_4</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_5</span><span class="p">])</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>The only new thing here is our language model. We use an incredible library called <a href="https://github.com/UKPLab/sentence-transformers">Sentence Transformers</a>, which is excellent at producing sentence vectors from transformer-based language models, like BERT. The specific model we use does not matter too much, but every model excels in its own domain.</p>
<p>It depends on the type of data the model was trained/fine-tuned on. The model used is a modified and fine-tuned variant of BERT that happens to be suitable for our queries and documents.</p>
<p>For an optimal solution, you'd need to finetune a model on your own data.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">level4_rank</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[]</span>
    <span class="c1"># Calculate vector for query</span>
    <span class="n">query_vector</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">model</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">encode</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">document</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">index</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="c1"># Compare the query and document vectors</span>
        <span class="n">score</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">cosine_similarity</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query_vector</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;vector&quot;</span><span class="p">])</span>
        
        <span class="n">result</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">{</span><span class="s2">&quot;doc&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">document</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="s2">&quot;score&quot;</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">score</span><span class="p">}</span>
        <span class="n">results</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">append</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">result</span><span class="p">)</span>
    
    <span class="c1"># Sort results</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="nb">sorted</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">results</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">key</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="k">lambda</span> <span class="n">x</span><span class="p">:</span> <span class="n">x</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="s2">&quot;score&quot;</span><span class="p">],</span> <span class="n">reverse</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="kc">True</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">return</span> <span class="n">results</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>All we do here is calculate a vector for our query and compare its similarity with every document vector.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;animal clips&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>silly dog videos               0.5840
Funny videos about cats        0.4831
footage about toy animals      0.3808
Cats, cats, cats!              0.3181
viral triple rainbow clip      0.3005
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;cat videos&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Funny videos about cats        0.7001
silly dog videos               0.5884
Cats, cats, cats!              0.4314
footage about toy animals      0.3773
viral triple rainbow clip      0.2705
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;videos of animals&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>silly dog videos               0.6273
Funny videos about cats        0.6228
footage about toy animals      0.4136
Cats, cats, cats!              0.4081
viral triple rainbow clip      0.2008
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Toy animals are no longer on top, and cat videos are about cats, not dogs. Awesome!</p>
<p>Looking closer at the scores, it seems that anything above <code>0.4-0.45</code> is relevant. This makes it easy to detect when we don't have anything good to show.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;raising a cat&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Funny videos about cats        0.3356
Cats, cats, cats!              0.3231
silly dog videos               0.2472
footage about toy animals      0.1689
viral triple rainbow clip      0.1097
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Even if the results contain the right keyword, it does not always mean that they are relevant. That's what we hoped to achieve.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h4 id="Open-source-implementation">Open-source implementation<a class="anchor-link" href="#Open-source-implementation"> </a></h4>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>To reach this point, we have done plenty of significant work.</p>
<p>As you can expect, if we wanted to productionise this, we would need to do even more. Ensuring that large language models scale smoothly is no easy feat.</p>
<p>This is something, we have worked hard on at Kiri.</p>
<p>We recently released our open-source <a href="https://github.com/kiri-ai/kiri">Natural Language Engine</a> that among other things makes building an intelligent search engine incredibly easy.</p>
<p>What's more, it works in over 50 languages. Let's have a look.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="kn">from</span> <span class="nn">kiri</span> <span class="kn">import</span> <span class="n">Kiri</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">Document</span>

<span class="c1"># Take your search to production with our API</span>
<span class="n">kiri</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">Kiri</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">api_key</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s2">&quot;abc&quot;</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="c1"># Or run locally</span>
<span class="n">kiri</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">Kiri</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">local</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="kc">True</span><span class="p">)</span>

<span class="n">documents</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="p">[</span><span class="n">Document</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">d</span><span class="p">)</span> <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">d</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="p">[</span><span class="n">doc_1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_2</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_3</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_4</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">doc_5</span><span class="p">]]</span>
<span class="c1"># Equivalent to our &quot;index&quot; functions</span>
<span class="n">kiri</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">upload</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">documents</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="k">def</span> <span class="nf">kiri_search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">):</span>
    <span class="n">results</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">kiri</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">query</span><span class="p">)</span>
    <span class="k">for</span> <span class="n">result</span> <span class="ow">in</span> <span class="n">results</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">results</span><span class="p">:</span>
        <span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">f</span><span class="s2">&quot;</span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">result</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">document</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">content</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s2"> </span><span class="se">\t</span><span class="s2"> </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">result</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">score</span><span class="si">:</span><span class="s2">.4f</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s2">&quot;</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">expandtabs</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="mi">30</span><span class="p">))</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">kiri_search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;videos of animals&quot;</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Funny videos about cats        0.4718
silly dog videos               0.4095
footage about toy animals      0.3761
Cats, cats, cats!              0.2332
viral triple rainbow clip      0.1327
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>Combining this approach with BM25 (for example with Elasticsearch) is how you can achieve state of the art search results. If this is something you're interested in, check out an example <a href="https://github.com/kiri-ai/kiri/blob/main/examples/core_functionality/Search.ipynb">here</a>.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h4 id="Level-4-limitations">Level 4 limitations<a class="anchor-link" href="#Level-4-limitations"> </a></h4>
</div>
</div>
</div>
    {% raw %}
    
<div class="cell border-box-sizing code_cell rendered">
<div class="input">

<div class="inner_cell">
    <div class="input_area">
<div class=" highlight hl-ipython3"><pre><span></span><span class="n">search</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s2">&quot;everything except animal videos&quot;</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_rank</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">level4_index</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>

    </div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="output_wrapper">
<div class="output">

<div class="output_area">

<div class="output_subarea output_stream output_stdout output_text">
<pre>Funny videos about cats        0.5970
silly dog videos               0.5647
Cats, cats, cats!              0.4448
footage about toy animals      0.3932
viral triple rainbow clip      0.3138
</pre>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>

</div>
    {% endraw %}

<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>You may think, "hold on now, that's not how you search!". To which I would say, why not?</p>
<p>Just because we are used to searching in one way, who's to tell a different way isn't better? I know what I want to find, but I am being forced to do it in a way the search engine likes, not the way I like.</p>
<p>The way we search is just a limitation of our technology, and the next level is all about advancing it.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<h3 id="Level-5---Broader-to-General-Intelligence">Level 5 - Broader to General Intelligence<a class="anchor-link" href="#Level-5---Broader-to-General-Intelligence"> </a></h3>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="cell border-box-sizing text_cell rendered"><div class="inner_cell">
<div class="text_cell_render border-box-sizing rendered_html">
<p>If you have noticed the trend, then with each level we have become more and more general.</p>
<p>We started with individual keywords that couldn't match singular, plural forms and compound words.</p>
<p>We moved on to token matching that could do the aforementioned.</p>
<p>Token matching was limited in understanding similar words and made some errors due to, well, the nature of token matching.</p>
<p>We introduced first traces of intelligence with word vectors that captured some meaning. Where we fell short was the relationships <em>between</em> words.</p>
<p>Logically enough, we upgraded and captured the meanings of word relationships. Our shortage here was a lack of knowledge about other documents and a "one approach that fits all" solution. While we improved the results, we definitely didn't solve search.</p>
<p>Where to from here? What we need is more intelligence, and specifically, broader intelligence.</p>
<p>Think about why you or anyone else searches.</p>
<p>Is it to find information?</p>
<p>Doesn't it make sense to find exactly what you're looking for, instead of finding traces that you need to put together manually?</p>
<p>Say you are trying to find how many different animal species there are or how many customer complaints your company has received in the last quarter.</p>
<p>You will be successful only if that data is already analysed. That is, someone has written a paper on the topic or put together a formal report. In many cases, the information is already there! You need the right intelligence to analyse it.</p>
<p>From here on, your imagination is the limit. The world is full of unstructured data like text.</p>
<p>What gems may we find and what problems could we solve, given an intelligence that analyses the world's information and connects the right bits?</p>
<p>For the time being, there isn't a solution.</p>
<p>However, making state-of-the-art AI more accessible is our mission at <a href="https://kiri.ai/">Kiri</a>. Once level 5 search is possible, we'll be sure to make it easy to use.</p>

</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>
